---
title: "Methods & Data"
---

## Corpus construction

### Papers

Academic papers were retrieved from [OpenAlex](https://openalex.org) using a two-layer keyword strategy:

**Layer 1 — core keywords** retrieve papers explicitly about the field:
> *synthetic biology · synthetic genomics · synthetic genome · BioBrick*

**Layer 2 — subfield keywords** extend coverage to technical papers that may not use "synthetic biology" explicitly:
> *synthetic gene network · synthetic gene circuit · synthetic cell · minimal genome · minimal cell ·
> standard biological parts · repressilator · xenobiology · cell-free synthetic biology*

This approach follows Shapira et al. (2017), who used a similar multi-keyword strategy
to delineate the synthetic biology literature.

All papers were filtered to years 2000–2026. The final corpus contains **8,720 papers**.

### iGEM Projects

iGEM project data were retrieved from the [iGEM Registry API](https://parts.igem.org).
Each project entry includes team name, institution, year, wiki description, and (where
available) a list of BioBrick parts used. Projects were normalized to a shared schema with
`id`, `title`, `text`, `year`, `city`, `country`, `lat`, `lon`, and `case_study_flag`.

The final corpus contains **4,615 projects** spanning 2009–2025.

### Patents

Patent data was not included in the final analysis. An initial attempt to retrieve
patents from Lens.org via IPC classification (C12N, C12P, C12Q) returned too few
usable records with city-level geocoding to support city-level analysis. This is
noted as a limitation.

---

## Geocoding

**Papers** were geocoded using the institution metadata returned by OpenAlex. Each
paper's lead institution was mapped to a city and coordinates.

**iGEM projects** were geocoded using a three-tier pipeline:

1. OpenAlex institution lookup (for recognized university names)
2. Research Organization Registry (ROR) for remaining institutions
3. Claude Haiku (local LLM) for hard cases with unusual institution names

All geocoding results were cached to disk and are reproducible without API calls on re-runs.
Cities with no geocoding result were excluded from the city-level analysis.

---

## Embeddings

All artifacts (papers and projects) were encoded into 768-dimensional vectors using
**SPECTER2** ([Specter2, 2022](https://huggingface.co/allenai/specter2)), specifically
the **proximity adapter** trained for symmetric document-similarity retrieval.

**Why SPECTER2?**

- Trained on scientific papers using citation-informed contrastive learning, making it
  more domain-appropriate than general models (BERT, GPT) for scientific text.
- The proximity adapter is explicitly optimised for measuring cosine similarity between
  two documents, which is exactly our use case.
- 768-dimensional output is compact enough to embed 13,000+ documents in a single session.

Each document was encoded as the `[CLS]` token of the final hidden layer, truncated
to 256 tokens (sufficient for most abstracts). Embeddings were cached incrementally
to disk — safe to interrupt and resume.

---

## Dimensionality reduction and clustering

### UMAP projection

Embeddings were reduced from 768 to 2 dimensions using
[UMAP](https://arxiv.org/abs/1802.03426) (McInnes et al., 2018).
Parameters: `n_neighbors=10`, `min_dist=0.05`, `metric=cosine`.

A separate 30-dimensional UMAP projection (same parameters) was computed for
clustering, since HDBSCAN performs better on higher-dimensional UMAP output
than on 2D (Grootendorst, 2022, *BERTopic*).

### HDBSCAN clustering

Clusters were identified with [HDBSCAN](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-642-37456-2_14)
(Campello et al., 2013) on the 30D UMAP coordinates.
Parameters: `min_cluster_size=10`, `min_samples=1`.

This produced 347 clusters. The 25 largest were labeled using Claude Haiku: for each
cluster, the 20 titles closest to the cluster centroid in 30D space were sent to the
model with a prompt asking for a 3–6 word topic label. Artifacts in smaller clusters
are grouped as "Other" in figures.

---

## City-level semantic overlap

For each city with at least one paper and at least one project with a valid embedding,
we computed:

$$\text{semantic\_overlap}_i = \cos(\bar{e}^{\text{papers}}_i,\; \bar{e}^{\text{projects}}_i)$$

where $\bar{e}$ is the L2-normalised mean embedding vector (centroid) for all
documents of that type in city $i$.

The centroid-similarity approach is equivalent to the mean pairwise cosine similarity
when embeddings are unit-normalised (Turney & Pantel, 2010), and is computationally
efficient for cities with many documents.

A city must appear in both the papers and projects datasets to contribute to the
city-level analysis. **379 cities** meet this criterion.

---

## Regression specification

We regressed city-level semantic overlap on:

$$\text{overlap}_i = \alpha + \beta_1 \log(n\_papers_i) + \beta_2 \log(n\_projects_i) + \beta_3 \cdot \text{year\_span}_i + \varepsilon_i$$

Count variables are log-transformed to account for the skewed distribution of
city sizes (a common practice in urban economics; Wooldridge, 2019, Ch. 6).
Standard errors are HC3 heteroskedasticity-robust (White, 1980).

---

## Limitations

1. **No patents.** The patent pipeline failed to produce a usable city-level dataset.
   Claims about the "papers → patents" step in the innovation trajectory cannot be tested.

2. **SPECTER2 is trained on papers, not projects.** iGEM project descriptions are shorter,
   less formal, and structurally different from scientific abstracts. The embedding model
   may represent papers more faithfully, potentially inflating paper-project similarity
   for short project descriptions.

3. **Cross-sectional design.** All years are pooled. We cannot observe temporal ordering
   within cities — whether projects preceded papers, or vice versa.

4. **Single-institution geocoding.** Papers with multiple author institutions are assigned
   to the lead institution only. Multi-city collaborations are not captured.

5. **Keyword inclusion rules.** The corpus definition depends on keyword lists. The choice
   of keywords determines which papers and projects are included, and reasonable alternatives
   would produce a different corpus.

---

## Reproducibility

All code is on [GitHub](https://github.com/Zer0Juice/synbiomap).
The full pipeline can be reproduced with:

```bash
# 1. Install dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt

# 2. Set API keys
cp .env.example .env  # edit to add your keys

# 3. Ingest papers and projects
python scripts/01_ingest_papers.py
python scripts/03_ingest_projects.py

# 4. Embed all artifacts (~6 hours on CPU, ~1 hour on Apple Silicon)
python scripts/04_embed.py

# 5. Project and cluster
python scripts/05_cluster.py

# 6. Label top 25 clusters (requires ANTHROPIC_API_KEY)
python scripts/05b_label_clusters.py

# 7. Export website data and figures
python scripts/06_visualize.py
python scripts/07_export_figures.py

# 8. Build the website
quarto render website/
```

Intermediate data files are cached; the pipeline is safe to interrupt and resume.
Pre-computed outputs (embeddings, clusters, city-level CSV) are included in the repository
so that steps 1–6 can be skipped for a quick local render.

---

## References

- Campello, R., Moulavi, D., & Sander, J. (2013). Density-based clustering based on hierarchical density estimates. *ECML PKDD*.
- Cohan, A., et al. (2020). SPECTER: Document-level representation learning using citation-informed transformers. *ACL 2020*.
- Grootendorst, M. (2022). BERTopic: Neural topic modeling with a class-based TF-IDF procedure. *arXiv:2203.05794*.
- McInnes, L., Healy, J., & Melville, J. (2018). UMAP: Uniform manifold approximation and projection for dimension reduction. *arXiv:1802.03426*.
- Shapira, P., et al. (2017). Mapping synthetic biology. *New Biotechnology*.
- Turney, P. D., & Pantel, P. (2010). From frequency to meaning: Vector space models of semantics. *JAIR*.
- White, H. (1980). A heteroskedasticity-consistent covariance matrix estimator. *Econometrica*.
- Wooldridge, J. M. (2019). *Introductory Econometrics*. Cengage.
